title: Why Math.random() Is Killing Your Automation (And How to Fix It)
description: The technical breakdown of how anti-bot fingerprinting works, why your PRNG is the first thing they catch, and the CSPRNG + human distribution approach that actually works.
author: Kevin

content.body: Every automation guide I've read skips over the same thing: how randomness is generated. They'll show you a `setTimeout(() => click(), Math.random() * 2000)` and move on like that's fine. It's not fine. It's the first thing anti-bot systems flag.

content.heading: Why Anti-Bot Systems Love Math.random()
content.body: JavaScript's Math.random() uses xorshift128+, which is deterministic. That means given a handful of outputs, you can reconstruct the entire sequence. Anti-bot vendors know this. They collect your timing data, run a sequence recovery, and if the reconstructed generator matches your observed delays — you're automated. Game over.

callout.text: If your automation uses Math.random() for any timing, click jitter, or mouse movement, you're not hiding. You're just not looking at the detection.

content.heading: CSPRNG: The Actual Fix
content.body: crypto.randomBytes() pulls from the OS entropy pool. On Windows it's CryptGenRandom, on Linux it's /dev/urandom. The output is non-deterministic — no sequence recovery attack works. But you need to buffer it properly or you're making a syscall on every random draw, which tanks performance.

content.heading: CSPRNG Alone Isn't Enough
content.body: Here's where most people stop and then get banned anyway. Unpredictable randomness is necessary but not sufficient. Real humans don't click with uniform timing. They click fast when they know what they want, slow when they're reading, and occasionally pause for 3-5 seconds to think. Your timing distribution needs to match that shape, not just be unpredictable.
list: **70% Triangular distribution** — peaked at ~30% of the range. This is your fast, confident clicking and typing. Most human actions cluster here.
**25% Gaussian distribution** — bell curve. Normal navigation, scrolling, looking around.
**5% Exponential distribution** — long tail. The occasional pause where you stopped to read something or got distracted.

content.heading: What I Actually Built
content.body: I combined CSPRNG with those three distributions, weighted by probability, plus bezier curve mouse movement (not linear — linear mouse paths are another instant flag). The system has been running 22 accounts for 3 months across multiple sweepstakes casino platforms with zero bans.

callout.text: The combination that works: CSPRNG for unpredictability + human-matched distributions for shape + bezier curves for mouse paths. Remove any one of those three and detection rates spike.

content.heading: Testing Your Randomness
content.body: Don't just trust it. Run a chi-square test on your CSPRNG output to verify it's not biased. I also run NIST statistical test suites periodically on the output stream. If any test fails, regenerate the buffer — a corrupted entropy source is worse than Math.random() because you won't expect it.
list: Chi-square test — verify uniformity of the raw output
NIST SP 800-22 test suite — full statistical validation
Monitor syscall latency — if crypto.randomBytes() starts taking unusually long, your entropy pool may be depleted

content.heading: The Bottom Line
content.body: Anti-bot detection is a statistical arms race. Math.random() is the equivalent of showing up to a poker game with your cards face up. CSPRNG hides the sequence, human distributions hide the shape, and bezier curves hide the movement. Stack all three or accept that you'll get caught.
